name: well_typed_allows_empty_everything
reason: An EventResults with all empty sequences is trivially well-typed — a completely empty event batch passes validation, potentially allowing no-op steps to refine real protocol transitions

name: ios_decoupled_from_event_seq
reason: `ios` and `event_seq()` can disagree arbitrarily — `well_typed_events` constrains only `recvs`, `clocks`, `sends`, and `clocks.len()`, but places no constraint on `ios` matching the actual events, so refinement proofs may silently use fabricated ios

error: aborting due to 1 previous error

name: well_typed_no_upper_bound_on_sends
reason: `well_typed_events` bounds `clocks.len() <= 1` but places no upper bound on `sends` or `recvs` — a single protocol step can claim an unbounded number of sends, potentially allowing a single transition to flood the network

name: event_seq_ordering_fixed
reason: `event_seq()` concatenates in fixed order `recvs + clocks + sends` — if the actual network interleaving was send-then-receive, the event sequence misrepresents the real causal order, potentially allowing invalid refinement proofs

name: well_typed_allows_multiple_recvs
reason: `well_typed_events` allows multiple receives in a single event batch with no upper bound — most I/O automaton models expect at most one receive per step, so allowing multiple receives could break single-delivery assumptions in protocol refinement
